{{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] revision.text:
{{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == Related Topics == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] revision.text:
{{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] (dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun (能褒野王塚古墳) | Cluster = Nobono Kofun Cluster (能褒野古墳群) | image = [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Full view of the tumulus (foreground right is the front section, background left is the rear section) | location = Tamura Town, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coordinates|34|53|4.36|N|136|28|55.09|E}} | ISO = JP-24 | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus (前方後円墳) | Dimensions = Total length: 90 meters
Height: 8.5 meters (rear mound) | Construction_period = Late 4th century | Burial_facility = (Presumed) {{ill|Vertical stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} | Buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} | Excavated_items = {{ill|Cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}, figurative haniwa | Imperial_tomb = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓) | Special_notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Label = Nobono Ōtsuka
Tumulus | Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-Southeast }} [[File:Nobono Otsuka Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono no Haka
(dedicated to Yamato Takeru)}}]] '''Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun''' (能褒野王塚古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kameyama, Mie|ja|亀山市}}, Mie Prefecture, Japan. The tumulus is part of the {{ill|Nobono Kofun Cluster|ja|能褒野古墳群}}. Although the identity of the buried individual is not known, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the tumulus as "Nobono no Haka" (能褒野墓), the resting place of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, a legendary prince and warrior of the 12th Emperor {{ill|Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}. It is also referred to as "Chōji-zuka" (銚子塚). This is the largest tumulus in northern Ise, measuring 90 meters in length, and is thought to have been built at the end of the 4th century (early Kofun period).The largest tumulus in Mie Prefecture is the Mihakayama Kofun (188 meters, Iga City), while the largest in Ise is the Takarazuka No.1 Kofun (111 meters, Matsusaka City). == Overview == [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Nobono Shrine (能褒野神社)}}]] The tumulus is located on the western edge of a plateau on the northern bank of the confluence of the Mitegawa and {{ill|Anrakugawa River (Mie)|ja|安楽川}}, tributaries of the Suzukagawa River. Surrounding the tumulus are more than ten kofun that together form the Nobono Kofun Cluster. The tumulus, previously called "Chōji-zuka" due to its resemblance to a sake bottle (銚子), was designated as a royal burial site by the Imperial Household Ministry (now Imperial Household Agency) in 1879. Although surveys were conducted in 1929 by the Imperial Forestry Bureau and in 2013 by the Imperial Household Agency's Office of Mausolea, the main tumulus has not been excavated. The tumulus has a keyhole shape, with the front section oriented southeast. Its total length is 90 meters, making it the largest tumulus in northern Ise. The structure exhibits transitional features between early and middle Kofun period designs. The rear section is constructed in two or three tiers, while the front section is built on a single tier. Excavated fragments of haniwa, including cylindrical and figurative haniwa, were found on the surface. A surrounding moat and embankment have been identified but were reconstructed in 1880, obscuring the original layout. The burial chamber is presumed to have been a vertical stone chamber, as local traditions recount that a vertical shaft large enough for several people was visible before the site's designation.[https://shoryobu.kunaicho.go.jp Imperial Household Agency Mausoleum Bulletin, No. 66, 2015] == Dimensions == The tumulus dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length''': 90 meters * '''Rear mound''': Two or three tiers ''' Diameter: Approximately 54 meters ''' Height: 8.5 meters * '''Front section''': Single tier ''' Length: 40 meters ''' Width: 40 meters ''' Height: 5.5 meters == Artifacts == Artifacts found at the tumulus include several haniwa fragments, particularly cylindrical and figurative haniwa. These haniwa, including those preserved at the Nobono Shrine, indicate cultural exchanges along routes from Iga (e.g., Ishiyama Kofun in Iga City) to northern Ise, leading to regions in Gifu (e.g., Hirui Ōtsuka Kofun in Ōgaki City) and Aichi (e.g., {{ill|Aotsuka Kofun|ja|青塚古墳}}). == Buried Individual == The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} designates the tumulus as the burial place of Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure from the 4th century.宮内省諸陵寮編 [https://ndl.go.jp/1237123/9 "陵墓要覧"] However, archaeological evidence does not confirm this association. The tradition linking the tomb to Yamato Takeru appears to have been solidified in the 19th century. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} * {{ill|Nobono Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}} * {{ill|White Bird Mounds (Shiratori)|ja|白鳥陵}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kameyama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]]
